One to Many
Selling
Presentations
How to Assemble 	the Most Effective
Group Selling Presentations for
In-Person Delivery or Media

With 	Dan 	Kennedy

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	4
┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
One to Many Selling 	Presentations: 	The Open
In this program, we're dealing with the architecture of group selling
presentations.
Pretty much it doesn't matter, although we will talk some specifics about 	stage
to group, webcast 	to group 	and DVD sent in the mail to group.
There 	are 	some nuance differences, we will get to them 	but, generally speaking,
the structure elements don't change that much, if at all, depending on delivery.
A group presentation is a group presentation 	is a group presentation 	and there
are things that help it 	structurally.
We will go through it beginning, middle, end and really be talking about
architecture.
Group Presentation: The Open
We 	begin 	with The Open. 	We'll go through these 	in detail; there's a short list of
objectives that should occur in the opening of a
presentation where anybody has the opportunity
to stop it.
In one-to-one selling, if you're at somebody's
kitchen table, face-to-face, and you've unpacked
the pot and pan samples all over the table, they
really don't have an opportunity to stop that
sales presentation abruptly and early unless they
are going to be incredibly rude.
Most of the time that we're selling one-to-one, we have a little looseness at the
beginning to establish rapport and have small talk.
There's a lot of things we can do one-to-one that we cannot get away with in any
environment where they have an opportunity to abruptly stop it.
In one-to-one selling,
they really don't
have an opportunity
to stop that sales
presentation
abruptly and early.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 37 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	5
┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
´éĚ 	If they're in a group, 	they can get up and leave.
´éĚ 	They 	can mentally tune out even if they are sitting there.
´éĚ 	If they have a clicker in their hand, 	they can obviously click.
´éĚ 	If they're online, they can go elsewhere online.
You have to be really sensitive to the idea that you can lose
them very early and never really have an opportunity.
I have people bring me scripts all the time and the good stuff starts 	eight
minutes in.
They will say, 	ÔÇťYeah, but when they get hereÔÇŽ.ÔÇŁ
I say they are not going to get
there.
They have too 	many options to
checkout.
Purpose of the Open
The first job of the 	Open is to
stop 	the 	listener 	/ 	viewer 	/
audience 	member 	from
checking 	out 	because 	their
earliest 	impulse 	is 	often 	to
checkout.
There's things you can do to make that worse by the way, and there's things you
can do to help stop it.
Second, we want to engage the listener/viewer and make them think, make
them start questioning, asking questions.
We want to frame the presentation, the discussion.
The Open
Purposes
´âś 	To STOP the listener or viewer 	from
actually OR mentally ÔÇťclicking outÔÇŁ
´âś 	To ENGAGE the L/V and make him
THINK (Often beginning with ?ÔÇÖs they ask
themselves)
´âś 	To FRAME THE DISCUSSION
´âś 	To SHORT-CIRCUIT INSTANT
ÔÇťNEGATIVESÔÇŁ
´âś 	To CREATE 	POSITIVE 	INTEREST
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 37 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	6
┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
We want to short-circuit instant negatives 	ÔÇô 	because they will tune out instantly
if something is put in front of them that they have a visceral negative reaction to
and 	we want to create positive interest.
That's all the stuff we want to do pretty quick in the 	Open of a group
presentation.
#1: 	What Must Be Established?
So, what must be established in an 	Open?
Who is Talking to Them
The first thing that has to be established, if they don't already know it, is who is
talking to them.
Even if they know it, we ought not take for granted that they know it.
Within the timing of the 	Open, we need to zip through as many of these things as
possible so that they are reminded, even if they knew it, that this is who's
presenting to them.
In 	selling anything to anybody, but the more you climb the
affluence ladder the more important it is, really the 	ÔÇśwho you areÔÇÖ
is more important than 	ÔÇśwhat you sellÔÇÖ.
People make more decisions based on 	ÔÇśwhoÔÇÖ 	than on 	ÔÇśwhatÔÇÖ.
These are all the things that you
could be:
Celebrity; somehow, as early as
possible, if you are famous, 	you
want to remind them that you
are famous.
Now, we have, environmental 	/
situational things we can do.
The Open
What Must Be 	ESTABLISHED?
WHO is talking to them?
1. Celebrity
2. Authority
3. Credibility
4. Affinity
5. Likeability
6. Role/Voice/Personality
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 37 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	7
┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
If we're doing it in a room, you have to give some thought about how would a
celebrity approach this room.
When I was speaking 	and 	selling 	from 	the platform 	ÔÇô 	even in small, not Peter
Lowe venues, but the Kansas Association of 	Whatsits 	and there's 50 people in
the room 	ÔÇô 	almost without exception, I did not hang out as people were coming
in the door, greet them, talk to them and make nice to them because that's not
what a celebrity does.
A celebrity is backstage and appears onstage when it's time for them to perform.
Furthermore, they usually have an opening act.
Ideally, somebody else is up there for 15 minutes
doing something before it's me.
You have to think about what you can do in
whatever media environment you are in that
telegraphs 	you're 	a 	celebrity 	and/or 	avoids
undermining the idea that you are a celebrity.
If you look at infomercials for example, 	where our time is precious; 	we have
28.5 minutes, that's all we've got. 	You can't stretch it, you can't do anything
about it.
You've got 28.5 minutes, you've got 	two call-to-actions in the middle and a short
one at the end, so really you're down to about 24 minutes, 	no matter what.
You've got to divide it into three segments. The first segment is where you have
to do all this establishing stuff. You get to abbreviate it at the start of the second
segment and the third segment, so really your selling time in the first segment is
the shortest because you have to do this.
If you go all the way back and you look at 	ÔÇťThink and Grow Rich,ÔÇŁ 	ÔÇŽ and at the
time of Think and Grow Rich in '87, most buyers 40 years and up would
instantly know who Fran Tarkenton isÔÇŽ 	but you can't take that for granted.
A celebrity is
backstage and
appears onstage
when 	it's time for
them to perform.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 7 of 37 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	8
┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
If you're paying money for Fran, you can't just stick Fran out there and assume
everybody knows who he is.
You've got to 	give up a couple minutes of that 	Open to establishing he's a
celebrity, he's a famous athlete, all the stuff they need to know about him.
If you are, or whoever it is you're presenting is, 	an 	authority, you've got to get
that across somehow too in the 	Open.
If they have enormous 	credibility 	in this subject area, you've got to get that
across.
If they have 	affinity 	with the audience, you've got to get that across.
Ben Glass is an attorney, he markets only to attorneys and he's driving only
attorneys to 	his webcast; 	still it's good to remind them that he's an attorney, not
a non-attorney, and that he's an extremely successful attorney.
That has to be done in that opening, no matter what was done before they got
there.
No matter how good a job you do in advance of it, think
of the presentation 	as happening in a vacuum.
As if they saw nothing before they got there; they heard nothing before they got
there.
They have fallen out of the sky into the room or
in front of the 	screen, and now, what do they
need 	to 	know 	for 	this 	presentation 	to 	be
effective?
If you sell by being likable 	ÔÇô 	not 	everybody does; some people sell, in fact, by the
opposite but 	most people are more effective if the people buying from them like
them 	ÔÇô 	then 	you have to figure out a way to establish 	likeability.
In 	Nixon's Checkers Speech, he carried the dog; there's a reason for that.
Most 	people are
more effective if the
people buying from
them like them.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 37 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	9
┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
Role, voice, personality; 	you have to establish a sense for them of who this is
talking to them that is going to be consistent throughout.
Some important cautions:
The 	better known you are to your audience, the less of this you must establish,
but you still ought not assume any 	of it as pre-existing. 	You can shorten 	it, but
you ought not eliminate any of it.
If you sell to the same people over and over again, you will be sorely tempted to
assume it; 	if for no other reason than you get tired of telling it.
But 	there's an enormous temptation to also believe they got it.
They know I grew up in a log cabin, I was raised by wolves... They already know
this becauseÔÇŽ so, there's this enormous temptation to think we don't need to
say it anymore.
Every time you do that, 	you will find it compromises your effectiveness because
they need this stuff re-established as a precursor to being sold to.
Even though we know each otherÔÇŽ if you go back and look at the brochure that
brought you here, there'd be a lot less people in the room if we 	left out all of the
reasons why you should listen to me about this.
Most of 	those reasons you already knew; 	but still, there's certain precursors to
selling you something that need to be said.
Why 	Are You 	Talking to Them (Now)?
We also must establish, why are you talking to them about this now?
You might think of this as relevance and as timely relevance.
There should be a reason why you are presenting this
and there should be a reason why you are presenting it now.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 37 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	10
┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
This argues against 	ÔÇťevergreenÔÇŁ 	presentations; however, if the person is new to
it and there's an 	evergreen timing reason, then the presentation can be
evergreen.
Often they can last for 	three or four years, but you should be sensitive to market
conditions, economic conditions, trauma going on in your marketplace.
In a general sense, we have bad economies, we have mediocre economies and
we have good economies. Most of the time the condition lasts at least for a few
years, so 	ÔÇťevergreenÔÇŁ 	is 	a 	fungible definition, but 	three or four 	years out of a
presentation is pretty good.
You're probably not going to get 10, 15 or 20 anymore out of a presentation and
the change in the economy, the change within an industry that you sell to may
require you to go tweak it.
Relevance is fine. You at least need that. You at least need sound reasons why
you are talking to them about X.
It's 	even 	better 	if 	you 	have 	a
timing 	relevance 	reason 	for
talking to them about X.
If for no other reason for that, you
may be a long-term thinker 	ÔÇô 	and,
if you are, 	great 	ÔÇô 	but most of the
people we sell to are very short-
term thinkers.
They have a pretty small frame of reference. 	They are into what is affecting
them today, this week, this month.
They look at the world through a pretty small window. If you're standing too far
to the side of the window, they don't see you at all, so timing relevance is useful.
WHY 	is He Talking to Them (Now)
Because of (e.g.)
´éĚ 	A current or impending event,
opportunity or danger (timing)
´éĚ 	A new and 	amazing ÔÇťbreakthroughÔÇŁ,
discovery, change in order of things
´éĚ 	Their circumstances, situation, coming
of a certain age
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 10 of 37 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	11
┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
ItÔÇÖs best if there's a current or impending event, or opportunity, or trauma that
you can hook your reason for being here talking to you about X now to. ThatÔÇÖs
great. That's maybe the best of the choices.
Next 	would 	be 	the 	new 	amazing 	discovery,
breakthrough, piece of technology; if you're in
health, the discovery 	that 	ÔÇťGround-up seaweed
taken in sufficient concentration, this has just
been found out in France by some doctorÔÇŽ.ÔÇŁ
that's sort of on the same par.
Sometimes you're there because they are reaching a certain life benchmark.
That can work for you, but it 	is best if you have reasons why and you establish
them early.
Why Should They Listen to 	You 	ÔÇô 	About This?
You have to establish why they should listen to you about this thing and, 	implied
in that, 	is why they should listen to you versus anybody else.
Most of us have comparables and competitors. 	Maybe I don't have a direct
competitor, but there are clearly other people you could listen to about almost
any subject that I ever talk about.
We have to hunt for the reason why you should listen to me versus all the
others, as well as why you should listen to me. Again, this has to be established
fairly quickly. In our infomercial world, it may be one sentence, but it is said.
If we stick Fran Tarkenton onto 	ÔÇťThink and Grow Rich,ÔÇŁ 	that only works if it's
said by him or by somebody, 	ÔÇťThe reason I'm so excited about talking to you
about Think and Grow Rich is because I'm a champion. I'm a winner. I've been all
about winning my entire life and I know what it takes to win, and here's 17 secrets
about what it takes to win.ÔÇŁ
Most 	of the people
we sell to are 	very
short-term thinkers.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 11 of 37 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	12
┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
If you don't do that, 	then you might as well have a completely irrelevant
celebrity up there because you haven't hooked them together. 	You need that
bridge early.
Why Should They Stay Tuned?
You need, 	very early, 	why they should stay tuned. 	A real common mistake is the
assumption that they will stay tuned.
Let's say it's your own seminar and they're coming to a physical room.
If 	somebody has registered to come see you, whether for free or pay, and they
have gotten in a car 	ÔÇô 	it's even better if they got
on an airplane of course 	ÔÇô 	but they have gotten in
a car and they have come to a physical place to
see you.
It's an easy assumption to make that they're
going to come in, sit down and they are going to stay tuned.
ItÔÇÖs a bad assumption because, 	generally speaking, 	people are badly behaved.
They don't have any discipline; they don't have any power of 	concentration.
They're easily distracted.
I was in an environment about two years ago, 	an 	audience about twice this size,
somebody else's audience but I'm the big dog of the day. 	People have come to
be there in the room ostensibly to see and hear me.
As I am walking up the side to come up on stage, 	I'm looking at the rows and
everybody's got their little doohickeys open and they are not at an anticipatory
Dan Kennedy site getting ready for the wonderful 	two 	hours they are going to
spend with Dan.
They are every place else you can think of. 	I looked at every row. 	They're
watching a movie; 	they're checking their social media; 	they're at Zappos buying
something; 	two porn sitesÔÇŽ
You need, very early,
why they should stay
tuned.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 37 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	13
┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
So, even in an environment where you have brought them to you and they have
come to you, there is still a bunch of competition for their attention 	ÔÇô 	including,
right at their fingertips, naked people; 	good-looking naked people.
I can't compete with that on any level.
I know they're trying not to stay tuned. 	In the Lowe events, as many of you
know, I was the last speaker of the day and there was a horrible stampede
because people stayed to see the last famous guy.
Whoever was before me, Colin Powell, General
Schwarzkopf, President Bush, whatever, in their
head, ÔÇťWe're going to stay for this last famous
personÔÇŽÔÇŁ 	in 	some cases only long enough to say,
ÔÇťWe saw him.ÔÇŁ
They're marching out during the last famous
person 	ÔÇô 	more for some than others 	ÔÇô 	but 	then,
when that last famous person is finishing, look
out.
I wasn't prepared for it the first time, but I started to prepare for it by the
second time because that's $297 going out the door with every one of those
people and there's a shitload of them and people could 	get hurt.
We really worked on how we 	could 	get them to stop, quickly re-think, ÔÇťDo I
want to leave?ÔÇŁ 	sit back down and stay tuned for a few minutes; still sitting hand
on bag, but 	ÔÇťStop!ÔÇŁ Now I've got a few-minute chance.
When they tune into a webcast, 	you've got a few-minute chance.
What can we do? Even if they came specifically to the webcast, you still only
have a few-minute chance.
The 	ÔÇťWhy they should stay tuned?ÔÇŁ 	you've got to figure out what that is and
everything that you can possibly do to 	help it.
Even in an
environment where
they have come to
you, there is still a
bunch of
competition for their
attention.
www.getwsodownload.com

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	14
┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
Elements to Stay Tuned
This is your list. One is, 	of course, 	quick promises.
The copywriting equivalent of this is the top one-third of the first page or of the
ad. It's the pre-head, the headline and the sub-head. That's what it is.
It's 	the three quick promises. 	I never like to script more than five because I
think people forgot the first one by the time you get to the fifth one; 	so three
quick promises that will stop somebody in their tracks.
That will 	get, ÔÇťOh, that's interesting,ÔÇŁ ÔÇťThat's exciting,ÔÇŁ ÔÇťI do want to hear that.ÔÇŁ
They literally are making the decision to, 	ÔÇťOkay, I'll give
him a little more time now,ÔÇŁ 	because of these promises.
You have a great 	personal relevance 	statement for them. 	If you have a tight
audienceÔÇŽ 	so if you had driven to a webcast out of a narrow source, and you
know 	age, 	demographic, 	pain
pointsÔÇŽ if you know these things
about your audience, 	then you
can 	craft 	the 	narrow 	ÔÇťIf 	you
areÔÇŽÔÇŁ 	statement, that the person
says, 	ÔÇťYes I amÔÇŁ 	to, then that buys
you 	some 	staying 	tuned 	for 	a
little while longer.
That's important; 	as a side point,
the broader the audience you put
yourself in front of, the more diverse the audience you put yourself in front of, 	to
make any kind of a group presentation, the less you have the ability to use that
tactic and the harder your job is.
If Ben can figure out how to drive only lawyers to a webcast who have been in
practice for at least 25 years, and have other attorneys working for them, and
have a family, 	and he knows that's the case, then 	ÔÇô 	because they don't need to
Elements to Stay Tuned
´éĚ 	Quick 	Promises 	ÔÇô 	3 to (max) 5
´éĚ 	Personal Relevance 	ÔÇô 	ÔÇťIf You AreÔÇŽÔÇŁ
´éĚ 	Provocative Question(s)
´éĚ 	Aroused Curiosity
´éĚ 	Breaking News
´éĚ 	Warning of Threat or Danger
´éĚ 	Specter of Loss
´éĚ 	Benefit Summary Statement
´éĚ 	Bribe(s)
´éĚ 	Direct, Specific Instruction

know he knows it 	ÔÇô 	he can say, 	ÔÇťIf you are an attorney who has been in practice
for no less than 20 years and 	you have attorneys working for you that frustrate

are, you especially need to stay tuned becauseÔÇŽÔÇŁ
If he's driven any and every attorney from the world to the same webcast, he
doesn't dare say it because he immediately gets the majority of the viewers to
tune out.
There's a link here 	from 	what audience you collect to whether or not you can
use this tactic, but it's a very powerful tactic.
Provocative 	questions, 	so 	you 	can 	frame 	your 	three 	promises 	more 	as
questions.

tuned.ÔÇŁ
´éĚ 	ÔÇťIf a magic genie appeared and offered you three wishes, and the wishes
were, boom, boom, boom, you need to stay tuned.ÔÇŁ
You can frame it as questions.
You can use pure 	curiosity. 	I don't like it much, but you do see it used where
they have been driven to a presentation not knowing what the thing is that
they're going to discover and the 	open 	still keeps it a mystery, but the curiosity
about it is about what it is and about the benefits it delivers.
ÔÇťIf there was a magic pill that you could get and you could take that instantly
eliminated body odor, bad breath, made you a charismatic individual, multiplied
your sex appeal and caused money to fall out of trees and hit you on the head,
would you like the pill?ÔÇŁ
You can do that kind of a magical, mystical approach.

-- 15 of 37 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	16
┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
Breaking news, 	if you are doing stuff live 	ÔÇô 	whether through web media,
human media, phone media, radio, TV 	ÔÇô 	then you have an opportunity to use
breaking news, but be careful.
It has to be breaking news that your audience knows about and, 	increasingly,
fewer and fewer people know about fewer and fewer things in the news.
It's only good breaking news if your audience knows
about it and is concerned about it.
So ÔÇťWho won American Idol last night?ÔÇŁ 	generally a whole lot more people know
that and are talking about that the next morning than know that we just invaded
Iraq for the fifth time. This 	a 	huge number will not even be aware of.
If you watched Leno at all in the late years of Leno 	ÔÇô 	and 	you 	paid attention 	ÔÇô
you saw year to year to year, more and more explaining of who or what he was
talking about to make sure that you got the joke.
If you go back and watch a Carson monologue, he never did it. There was a
consensus knowledge in America of who 	was President 	and 	who was Vice
President, for example, and who we may have invaded that month.
Everybody kind of knew that stuff and so Carson didn't have to explain much.
If you looked at Leno and you went and picked two shows from four years ago,
and two shows from three years ago, and two shows from two years ago and
you just look for this, you'll see that the amount of explaining of the setup of the
joke increased 	ÔÇô 	because he was accurately fearful that huge numbers of
viewers wouldn't have the reference for the joke.
If you want to talk about Biden, you can't say 	ÔÇťBidenÔÇŁ 	because, 	ÔÇťHuh?ÔÇŁ 	You've
got to 	say, ÔÇťThe current Vice President of the United States, Joe Biden.ÔÇŁ
You've got to do that now. So breaking news only works if you're certain it's a
news event that is news to the folks that you are selling to.
www.getwsodownload.com

In 	a niche market, you can't even assume; 	Craig can't even assume that real

I think that's a current economic fact 	ÔÇô 	in 	some markets more so than others 	ÔÇô
but you can't assume it.
They don't watch Fox Financial. 	They don't necessarily read their trade
journals. Eight of them in an office tell each other how things are going.
So if 	he's going to use breaking news that housing sales improved by 6.2% last
month 	ÔÇô 	the best improvement in a single month in four years 	ÔÇô 	that's very
relevant to selling to them, but he has to assume they don't know any of that.
He's going to have to take the time to say that, 	if
he's going to use that piece of breaking news to
get somebody to stay tuned.
Same sort of thing with a 	warning 	of threat or
danger. 	You can't assume.
I wrote a web PowerPoint presentation for a
client of mine to hospital executives about the
coming 	coding crisis caused by Obamacare.
It's okay if you don't know this because you don't run a hospital, but hospital
executives and medical records department directors and so 	forth should know
that, 	postponed by one year by executive orderÔÇŽ 	and I'm now not going to get
the exact number right but I'll be close... 	so, in October of next year, instead of
October this year, the codes that they use to bill Medicare and insurance
companies and so 	forth, go from roughly 40,000 codes to 246,000 codes. This is
like a mess. 	If you wait to deal with it until October of next year, you're going to
be el-screwed-o.
I fortunately 	have a person who has a medical background 	so, when 	I started to
work on this, 	I said make some phone calls and just try and get a sense for me of
Breaking news only
works if you're
certain it's a news
event that is news to
the folks you are
selling to.

how 	common 	knowledge 	this 	is 	with 	people 	running 	medical 	records
departments, and with 	the 	HRD person in a hospital.
The person sat on the phone for a day, came back and said, ÔÇťI wouldn't assume a
lot of knowledge about this if I were you. There's sort of a vague awareness that
this is going to change, but there's no real sense of the numbers.ÔÇŁ
Out of 22 completed conversations, 	four 	could quote some number of the size of
the coming crisis.
I've got a crisis to work with; it's a great crowbar, but I can't assume that there's
any knowledge about the crisis.
The 	specter of loss, that will keep somebody to stay tuned if you can scare the
shit out of them. 	ÔÇťStay tuned because they're coming to get your guns 	and 	IÔÇÖve
got new proof that they're coming to get your guns tomorrow.ÔÇŁ
If you put that in front of a bunch of gun owners, you've gained another 	three 	or
four 	minutes to hold their attention. They're not clicking out.
Benefit summary statement; 	so if you're going to lean on the benefit, then you
put the benefit in there.
If you look old or new 	at my scripts 	ÔÇô 	my 1999 Peter Lowe script 	or 	current
video sales 	scripts 	ÔÇô 	you'll see I'm living with the same structure. I haven't
changed from 1999.
The summary benefit tied to who you are 	was, 	ÔÇťIf you are a salesman, I'm going
to show you how to never have to cold prospect again.ÔÇŁ
That's a summary benefit of everything we do with 	Magnetic 	Marketing and
salespeople. The ultimate benefit is they don't have to do any prospecting. The
prospects come to them. 	If you're a business owner, it's a different summary
benefit.

Bribes; 	so you can bribe them to stay tuned. 	With teleseminars 	or 	webcasts, the
opening 	tease 	can 	include 	that, 	during 	the 	course 	of 	the 	presentation,
everybody's going to get a free e-book; everybody's going to get a free download
of a 17-point checklist that will immediately doubleÔÇŽ; 	that will immediately cut
their blood sugar level in half; 	that will immediately give them hard, firm
erections without having to take a pill.
ÔÇťYou want to stay tuned because we're going to let you have this for free.ÔÇŁ
You can bribe them with, 	ÔÇťEverybody who's on the call, sometime in the call, you
all have a code number that you got when you registered for the call and we're
going to have a drawing. We're going to call out the code number and somebody
is going to win a car.ÔÇŁ
So you can bribe them to stay tuned if you wish.
Finally, you want to give them the 	direct specific instruction. 	I rarely do a
stay-tuned statement without saying 	ÔÇťStay 	TunedÔÇŁ 	one way or another.
´éĚ 	ÔÇťYou want to make sure you have a pad, you want to make sure you have a
pen 	because you're going to want to take notes.ÔÇŁ
´éĚ 	ÔÇťYou want to stay tuned 	because 	in the next 22 minutesÔÇŽÔÇŁ 	[We often lie to
them about the time because 	ÔÇťin the next 22 minutesÔÇŽÔÇŁ 	and it's really
48.]
So 	you give them the specific instruction 	but you want to establish why they
need to stay tuned.
Established in Advance
Now, the more of what needs established that is established for you, the better.
So, here are some mistakes speakers make who go speak at other people's
events. 	They leave all the 	pre-event stuff up to the host.

┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
You're going to go speak 	at Keller 	Williams, and you deliver to them 	your
promotional 	stuff, 	ÔÇťHere's 	my 	biography, 	here's 	some 	articles, 	here's 	the
website,ÔÇŁ 	and then you're out until you show up.
Well, you're not going 	to get as much 	out 	of this as you could. First of all, they're
going to democratize it. 	If they have 18 speakers on a program, all 18 are
getting the same brochure real estate; 	all 18 are getting the same website real
estate.
If they're going to send out 18 emails, one about each speaker, they're all going
to be exactly the same length. If they have links, they're all going to have links; if
they don't have links, none of them are going to have links.
When you 	cede 	the control of 	what gets established about you
before you get an opportunity to make a presentation
to people, you don't get nearly 	as much
established as if you exercise a great
deal of control.
The 	only one who 	really 	cares about this being
done well is you, not them.
Even in environments where they're sharing in
revenue you create from the presentation 	ÔÇô 	be 	it
doing it live, or they're all driving as affiliates, the
association is driving people to your webcast 	ÔÇô
again, it doesn't make much difference.
Even when they're sharing in the revenue, they
care less about it than you do. In some cases they don't believe there's as much
revenue there to be had as you know there is if we do everything right.
They may delegate it to people who still don't care at all.
The more of what
needs established
that relies on you
getting it established
(vs. having it
established for you),
the harder the sales
job that follows is
going to be.

Revenue share doesn't affect Barbara to whom all this work got handed off to.
One of the things you will always see, if you are speaking for someone else at
their event 	ÔÇô 	or 	you are having them drive to a teleseminar or a webinar,
whatever 	ÔÇô 	and you give 	them generic information about you, they're going to
put out the generic information.
Nobody is going to sit and tweak it to be relevant to their audience.
If 	you 	look 	at 	our 	stuff, 	currently 	the 	Info-Summit 	magazine/brochure,
everybody sends generic stuff, everybody.
I have yet, in all the years I've been on the other side of that, we're running an
event, we hire a bunch of 	speakersÔÇŽ 	comma space 	speakers 	are coming,
including the speakers who are coming to sell, so they should care deeply about
what gets established for them before they get there.
They send their generic stuff. Ours is not a generic audience.
Now this time, if you look at the 	Info-Summit piece, you'll see I was able to do
less of it for Guber than I would normally do for the celebrity because, 	it turns

who limited what we could write about him
severely.

but we now have a team of sales prevention
attorneys in Chicago as well. 	There would be a
risk of the guy stiffing you and 	not showing and a problem.

pretty much any other celebrity, you'll see I didn't do as good a job. It's because
I couldn't do as good a job, but you'll see I still did a job.

themselves, and I wrote it so, 	to info-marketers in our world, there was
relevance.
No speaker does it from their side. 	I do it when I go speak. 	If I'm going to go
work for Greg Stanley and go do a dental group, I don't give them my generic
stuff. I go get doctor testimonials and I go get my health care references. I give
them promotional material about me that is directly relevant to their audience.
If I gave them all of it, so, ÔÇťHere's all the health care stuff, here's all the legal stuff
and here's all the real estate stuffÔÇŽÔÇŁ 	and I gave it to them all in a big box with the
generic stuff, they still wouldn't get it. 	They wouldn't do it.
The more of this that is established for you, and in advance of the sales event for
you, the better. But you don't want to rely on anybody else to do it well.
If you are on the other side of the equation, and so you are hosting a webinar for
Susan on how to use persuasive voice for real estate agents 	ÔÇô 	and 	you plan on
making money on that 	ÔÇô 	you can't settle for Susan giving 	you generic stuff about
Susan and you pumping 	it out to the real estate agents.
You've either got to make her go fix it, or you've got to say, 	ÔÇťOkay, I've got to
take this generic stuff and I've got to fix it to communicate to my people because
I've got to establish as much of this as possible for Susan before the time that
she begins delivering a presentation to my real estate agents.ÔÇŁ
I'm telling you, you will never see a speaker do it. I've yet to see a speaker do it.
A speaker 	sends me his two-pocket folder with the same stuff in it that he sends
to everybody, and the same CD with eight pictures on it that 	he sends 	to
everybody. That's what he sends.
Nine out 	of 	ten places he would then go work 	and 	would have shown what he
sent to the audience before they arrived.
He would not have as much established for him as if it was tweaked, which gets
us to 	adapting to the nature of an audience.

┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
#2: Adapting to the Nature of An Audience 	are we missing something?
#3: Putting the Required Items in Order
When you look at all this stuff that has to be in an 	Open, and all this stuff that
has to be established, you do have to consider the nature of the audience.
If it doesn't change what is said and what gets established, it may at least change
the order in which it is said and gets established because different order may
matter to different people.
First 	of 	all, 	customized 	presentations 	are 	of 	course 	best 	for 	customized
audiences.
The 	president and a vice president of a fairly large sales training company go
speak to a bunch of mailbox store owners, where you go pack and ship stuff.
They are selling a $90 a 	month coaching program on sales training.
Their presentation to these mailbox shop owners is, 	ÔÇťOver 12 months, we're
going to turn you into master salespeople so you'll be able to leave your shop, go
out, run around and see all the businesses in your 	area, walk in and sell them on
using your services.ÔÇŁ
This is the rough equivalent of
telling 	a 	roomful 	of 	nuns 	that
they will be much more effective
in the world if they just become
hookers. 	This 	is 	roughly 	the
same appeal.
They're in front of a roomful of
people 	and the reason these folks
own 	a 	pack 	and 	ship 	store 	is
because it's a store and it has a
counter.
Adapting to Nature of Audience
a) 	Difference between ÔÇťcoldÔÇŁ audience vs.
ÔÇťwarmÔÇŁ audience vs. members,
customers, fans
b) 	Difference between strangers to the
idea vs. those familiar with the idea
c) 	Difference 	between quality of source
d) 	Difference in age or other demographics
e) 	Difference between an audience
accustomed to being sold via the media
and way you are selling vs. an audience
for whom it is foreign
f) 	Difference between having them inside
four physical walls 	vs. CD/DVD vs. online
ÔÇô 	and if inside walls, time of day, what
preceded, etc.
www.getwsodownload.com

┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
That's its chief appeal is they get to come to the store and they get to stand
behind 	the 	counter, and people come in and give them money. 	That's the
business, that's what they want.
They really want the same people all the time because they really want to be
Ted Danson 	in 	Cheers. That's what they want, except they don't serve booze.
They want to be at the bar, and Norm comes in every day and sits there at the
end of the counter with them and has lunch with them. Barbara comes in every
Thursday with her Xerox copies to be made and they chat about Barbara's kid
and dog and whatever. That's what they want.
You know this 	is the deal because every one of these franchise owners gives
them an apron to wear. Hardly anybody goes out and makes sales calls wearing
an apron.
The guy comes in and he puts on his giant apron and he pulls his stool up. 	He
sits down and he waits for people to come in.
Telling 	him, ÔÇťYou're going to take off the apron and you're going to become a
master salesman, and you're going to put on a tie and you're going to go out and
you're going to walk in and sellÔÇŽÔÇŁ 	is 	a 	bad message to market match.
You see it done all the time by the way, not surprisingly.
Now there's a way for them to have killed. The kill is, 	ÔÇťHere's what we're going
to do. For 	the first time ever 	ÔÇô 	and you guys have probably tried it before and not
done very well 	ÔÇô 	you're going 	to be able to have a commission-compensated
outside salesperson who runs around and sells for you to all of the other businesses
in your community. Because 	we are going to make him effective for $90 a month.ÔÇŁ
That, everybody runs to the back of the room.
What they did, the only people that went to the back of the room went there in a
trickle during the presentation 	ÔÇô 	leaving out the back of the room.

-- 24 of 37 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	25
┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
Keep in mind, this is the CEO of the largest sales training company in America
who doesn't understand the fundamental message to market match.
I mean a chiropractor can effectively go out and knock on doors and make cold
calls, but it's a damnably 	hard-to-sell idea to chiropractors.
You've really got to give thought when you build your whole
presentation 	ÔÇô 	including your offer as well as your 	Open 	ÔÇô 	to 	who
is that audience and where are they in terms of
relationship to you?
There's a difference, first of all, between a 	cold audience and a warm
audience.
A cold audience is obviously tougher. 	Less has been established with a cold
audience, so you do have to work a little more at getting it right.
There's a difference between people being 	strangers to an idea 	and already
having familiarity with that idea, having some level of comfort with that idea.
In the 	ÔÇťGet 	RichÔÇŁ 	world, the longest running 	ÔÇťGet 	RichÔÇŁ 	opportunity pitch in the
history of the United States is 	ÔÇťGet 	Rich in 	Real 	Estate.ÔÇŁ 	It dates back to the Civil
War.
There were people putting people in rooms and selling them, 	ÔÇťHow 	to Get Rich
in Real EstateÔÇŁ 	during the Civil War. This is the oldest, uninterrupted, effective,
ÔÇťGet 	RichÔÇŁ 	pitch ever.
Why? 	Because 	everybody already has familiarity. 	They live in a house; 	they
hope to live in a house; they know people who live in houses.
Increasingly, once you were a little further along than the Civil War, almost
everybody knows somebody, the dumb uncle, who owns 	three or four rental
properties and, 	despite his bitching and moaning about the tenants, they know
the uncle is making money.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 25 of 37 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	26
┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
The next longest enduring, but much more difficult always to make work, has
been 	ÔÇťGet 	Rich in 	Mail 	OrderÔÇŁ 	because everybody has familiarity.
During the five decades it worked best, everybody got a lot of 	catalogs 	in the
mail, everybody did buy from mail order. Sears Wish Book was in everybody's
home, so you could do mail order pitches and there was already a sense of, 	ÔÇťI get
it.ÔÇŁ
It's why 	in ÔÇťGet Rich 	in Internet,ÔÇŁ 	specific 	Get 	Rich in Internet works better than
general 	Get 	Rich in Internet. 	So 	ÔÇťGet 	Rich on eBayÔÇŁ 	works better than 	ÔÇťGet 	Rich
on the InternetÔÇŁ 	because everybody has familiarity with eBay, that it works, and
they know some dumb uncle who is selling crap on eBay and somehow making
money even though he's an idiot.
If they have familiarity, you can deal with this differently
than if they don't have 	familiarity.
How you 	open, how critical your 	Open is, what you do with it, the 	quality of the
source 	matters a lot.
If we drive dentists to a webcast about 	Getting
Rich 	in 	Real 	Estate 	as 	the 	perfect 	second
opportunity for dentists, and we drive the great
unwashed masses to a webcast about how to 	Get
Rich 	in 	Real 	Estate 	for 	the 	great 	unwashed
masses, which person do you think we have a better chance with of keeping
them paying attention for the first ten minutes?
The dentist, by far.
First of all, the pitch says, ÔÇťIt's for you.ÔÇŁ
The other pitch has to say it's for everybody which diminishes the appeal of the
pitch.
If the pitch has 	to
say it's for everybody
that 	diminishes the
appeal of the pitch.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 26 of 37 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	27
┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
Secondly, the dentist has already learned how to 	pay attention. He had to go to
college. 	He had to sit in a class. He had to pass a test somehow in order to be
allowed to crawl into your mouth.
He has to do that every year to keep getting his certification. He actually has to
pay attention when he 	does his work, for the most part. He's got to have some
ability to focus. He probably reads more.
Charlie Brown who has got his GED, and works at the convenience store, he is
not as highly skilled in paying attention to anything. 	He's out in the first six
minutes of porn. I mean he just can't stay focused.
Source matters a lot. 	Who we are driving to our presentations affects how we
handle the 	Open.
Age or other demographics: 	Generally speaking, you've got to be much more
concerned about this 	Open with 25 year olds than you do with 55 year olds.
They're just less patient.
70 year olds will pay attention. 	Actually you
don't 	have 	to do much to keep them tuned
because they don't move around much.
I counted the other day just to be sure, so I have
137 	television channels 	ÔÇô 	counting premium and
Russian and Japanese, and every other thing.
I've got 137.
I've probably got about 120 that you might watch, because I won't watch the
Japanese station generally speaking. Every once in a whileÔÇŽ there's a home
shopping show on the Japanese channel. It's just fascinating.
You can't tell what anybody's saying, but it's really interesting to watch for a
little bit, to watch 	them copy what we do on home shopping. It's really sort of
interesting.
Who we are driving
to our presentations
affects how we
handle the Open.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 27 of 37 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	28
┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
So 	I have all these channels 	and 	I watch like 	six 	television channels. I have Sirius
radio in my car. 	I think there's 200 radio stations. 	I've got 	four. 	I would 	be
perfectly happy 	to just have the 	four.
They just did a big studyÔÇŽ the average homeowner has over 130 television
channels; they watch 17.
Seventy year olds watch 	four 	and they still set it for the night, like they used to
in the 50s.
In the 50s, whatever evening news you watched, all the way into the 60s, that's
pretty much where the TV channel stayed for the entire evening.
If you watched Walter Cronkite, you watched CBS until you went to bed, or
switched over to Carson. 	That's sort of what went on. 	Well they still behave
that way. 	So if they tune in, they stay tuned in. 	Twenty year olds, no.
You've got to think about age as you do your 	Open, and you've got
to think about the demographics, income level; 	all of that affects
their ability and their willingness to stay tuned.
You've got to think about the difference between an 	audience accustomed to
being sold by the media you are selling with, versus an audience for whom it
is foreign.
If you look at direct response advertising that sells to 55 and up 	ÔÇô 	so 	the in-
home walk-in bathtub, the mattress that 	moves up, the doohickey that gets you
up and down steps 	ÔÇô 	if 	you look at that advertising, none of it drives to webcast.
It all drives to, 	ÔÇťRaise your hand, we'll send you a DVD.ÔÇŁ
Don't think they haven't experimented.
These are big companies actually run by smart people. 	Don't think they
wouldn't love to eliminate the cost of the DVD.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 28 of 37 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	29
┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
However, their customers are far more accustomed to watching television than
they are to watching a computer, and they buy and own DVDs.
They are much more accustomed to taking a DVD and putting it in the TV than
they are anything else.
So you have to think about what they're accustomed to in terms of media.
There is 	some difference between 	inside physical walls 	and, 	if inside walls, the
time of day and what preceded it, and all of that. That's our 	Open.
#4: A Big Idea That ÔÇťSticksÔÇŁ
Then, we also have to worry about 	Open and 	Into 	Presentation.
We do usually want a big idea that has the ability to stick.
It's useful for there to be one overriding, big thing that everybody can get, either
visually, or said or stated, that is the umbrella for everything that is going to
happen.
Here's your checklist. You want it simple. You want it telegraphic.
A successful infomercial I worked on years ago, which I've now recycled, was
Gary Cochran's show, 	ÔÇťGet a Second Paycheck Without a Second Job.ÔÇŁ
You've got it. It's a big idea, and a person immediately gets it.
ÔÇťI go to work, I get a $200 a week
check 	for work. If I 	could get a
second 	one 	without 	getting 	a
second job, that'd be really cool.ÔÇŁ
They get it. 	That's a big idea that
sticks.
It's very simple, it's very direct.
It's not particularly 	informative, but it's very big and it's very quick.
A Big Idea That Sticks
VITAL POINT: PRIMACY AND RECENCY
´éĚ 	Simplicity By Exclusion
´éĚ 	Simplicity By Statement
´éĚ 	A Whack On The Side Of The Head
´éĚ 	Powerful Image / Word Picture
´éĚ 	Specificity
´éĚ 	Trigger 	An 	Emotion
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 29 of 37 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	30
┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
Simplicity by exclusion 	is leaving out a whole lot of detail and just getting to
the big idea and who it's for.
There's 	simplicity by statement, so again, one that we had on for 	eight 	years
was, 	ÔÇťTurn $399 	into $3990 Every Weekend.ÔÇŁ
A person gets it. 	A weekend, they know what a weekend is, and they know what
$399 is.
If you told them, 	ÔÇť10 	x 	$399 	Every 	Weekend,ÔÇŁ 	that they wouldn't get because
that requires them to do math and most people
are no good at math.
You have to say it for them, but that's simplicity
by statement.
A big 	whack on the side of the head. 	There's a
campaign running now, 	ÔÇťEven Poor People in
Okinawa 	Outlive 	Rich 	People 	in 	America
(Because of Their Diet.)ÔÇŁ
Now most people don't know where Okinawa is, but they generally know it's 	not
where they are. It's an exotic, foreign-sounding thing.
They probably instantly think of it as kind of a poor place because any place
that's not America to an American is a poor place.
These people are outliving us because of their diet 	ÔÇô 	not because of doctors,
hospitals and pills, but because of their diet. That's kind of a big whack.
Powerful image 	or word picture. 	Everybody used to laugh about it, but there
was an infomercial that ran, Saturday Night Live did a parody of it.
The guy eventually went to jail, but not because of the images; 	but there was a
very successful infomercial that ran for years about 	Get 	Rich in 	Real 	Estate.
It's useful for there
to be one overriding,
big thing that
everybody can get,
that is the umbrella
for everything.
www.getwsodownload.com

The guy's name was Tommy Vu and the show literally opened with Tommy in a
hot tub with four really hot babes in bikinis around him.
People laughed their ass off. 	The show, in 	its time, brought in about $100
million. It lasted for 	five years or so and everybody talked about it.
Either everybody saw it, or they were telling their buddies, 	ÔÇťYou've got to watch
for this infomercial because there's this little
Chinese guy who we all know couldn't possibly
get laid, and he's in this hot tub with these four
babes and it's all because he's getting rich.ÔÇŁ
This was like a national conversation. 	It was a
very powerful image.
You can get a lot of 	Open power if you figure out
how to do a big image as part of your 	Open.
Specificity, so 	specific promises, numbers, number of days; 	that can work as a
sticky idea.
Triggering one of the key emotions 	works. 	The most successful newsletter
promotion, maybe of all time but certainly of recent years, 	has been an
advertising campaign that drives to an online video for 	ÔÇťEnd of America.ÔÇŁ
Radio ads, TV ads, direct mail; 	this thing's huge and, 	in a slightly tweaked
incarnation, 	it is still running.
If you want to see the original, you can go to YouTube and find it.
EndofAmerica.com, that's a big sticky idea and it triggers alarm.
Dale 	Carnegie's old, 	ÔÇťHow to Win Friends and Influence People,ÔÇŁ 	was a big sticky
idea and it was about anti-embarrassment and a desire for popularity.

Open power if you

a big image as part
of your Open.

As you go to work on openings, by the way, I have a couple of resources for you.
The first one is something you can use day-in, day-out, as you create 	Opens.
It's 	Michael 	Masterson's book on Great Leads. Go to Amazon, get this book. This
will help you a lot with 	Opens.
Then, if you don't have 	ÔÇťHow to Write a Great Advertisement,ÔÇŁ 	it's the best
headline 	cheat. 	What is this?
There 	are 	150 headline templates in it which, 	if you 	are going 	to write three
opening statements, you're writing three headlines.
More conceptual is the 	ÔÇťMade to StickÔÇŁ 	book and the 	ÔÇťFascinateÔÇŁ 	book by
Sally 	Hogshead.
How to Do Ideas That Stick
Here's a bunch of different ways to do ideas that stick. One is by the 	numbers.
Numbers help people hold onto an idea. That's why you see them used so much.
You have 	ÔÇť17 Laws of Success,ÔÇŁ 	you have 	ÔÇť7 Habits of Highly Successful People.ÔÇŁ
A health talk is always difficult to do in the rally environment. 	For all but two of
the years that I was on the 	Success tour, we had a health speaker in the
afternoon. His name was Ted Broer. I'm sure you can Google Ted and probably
find presentations.
Ted did the 	ÔÇť10 Foods Never to EatÔÇŁ 	and it's a sticky idea. 	ÔÇťThere's 10 and I
should never eat them.ÔÇŁ
He 	was 	really 	effective 	and
others who tried in his subject
matter 	with 	us 	were 	not
particularly effective. It's a great
presentation.
Ted, for years, made a 	seven-figure income never leaving his house, just doing
radio interviews.
How to Do Ideas that Stick
´éĚ 	By the Numbers
´éĚ 	By Alphabet
´éĚ 	By Objects
´éĚ 	By Symbolism
´éĚ 	By Graphic / Dramatic Images 	space
www.getwsodownload.com

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	33
┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
He was very adept at 10 Foods Never to Eat, doing a radio interview, driving
people first to a phone room and then to a website, 	as time progressed, and
selling $400/$500/$600 boxes of information.
´éĚ 	ÔÇťFoods 	Never to 	EatÔÇŁ 	is nowhere near as impactful as sticking a number
on it, 	ÔÇť10 Foods Never to Eat.ÔÇŁ
´éĚ 	ÔÇťHabits of Highly Successful PeopleÔÇŁ 	is nowhere 	near 	as impactful as 	ÔÇť7
Habits of Highly Successful People.ÔÇŁ
You almost never see great advertising without a number in it.
Another way to make ideas stick is by 	alphabet 	and acronyms and we all cringe
at this, but it works. It helps people grasp an idea.
Objects. 	You see a lot of presenters over the years use an object to hold
attention and make an idea stick.
If you saw Zig speak, you almost never saw him speak without the big giant
pump 	on stage. 	That 	thing had to be shipped from place to place 	to place. 	It
actually was metal. It was heavy. I don't know what it cost in a year to ship that
damn thing from place to place to place.
For the Lowe events, he had to have two of them because we did Tuesdays 	and
Thursdays and you couldn't count on getting the thing from noon Tuesday to the
morning of Thursday, so there were two moving around the country all the time.
Zig was no dummy. 	He'd already figured out long ago he sold more stuff with
the pump than without the pump.
You can do a lot. You'll always see the weight loss people, the smart ones, doing
the big lump of rubber fat. 	ÔÇťThis is how much?ÔÇŁ 	That 	was done 30 years ago; 	I
saw Dr. Oz do it a few months ago.
Somewhere there's a company that makes big, giant rubber globs of fat that the
guy has probably gotten rich over the years and his kids are all clipping annuity
coupons because they've sold millions of these.
www.getwsodownload.com

┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
You'll see it done because people get it. So 	objects can help you a lot, physical
objects on stage, on a set, on a webcast set.
One of my gripes about webinars and webcasts is people don't build sets, but it's
TV so we should build sets.
Ideas that stick by 	symbolism. 	You may remember during the first campaign,
there's was some talk about a commercial in which Obama was speaking to you
from a house 	ÔÇô 	inside a little, normal, middle of the road, mainstream American
house 	ÔÇô 	and 	there's a window behind him and the window, it didn't have a cross
in it, it's just that the things that hold 	held 	the window together formed a cross.
It was right over his shoulder and it was lit. 	It was a little brighter than
everything else that was behind his shoulder. 	Most people didn't notice it
consciously.
The media picked up on it and then everybody talked about it, but there's only
three 	million people that pay attention to the media so it doesn't matter.
It was almost certainly done deliberately. 	At the time, as you know, he was
heavily dealing with, 	ÔÇťIs this guy a Muslim?ÔÇŁ
Whoever 	did 	it 	deliberately 	did 	it 	for 	good
reason. Symbolism matters a lot.
For many years, I guess probably until maybe the last 20, sort of a symbol of
having made it in America was driving a Cadillac.
Almost all 	ÔÇťGet RichÔÇŁ 	marketing of any kind used Cadillacs. 	Direct selling put
people in Cadillacs. Originally, Mary Kay was you didn't get a choice, you got a
pink Cadillac. 	You didn't get a Lincoln; 	you didn't get a Buick, you didn't get a
Prius.
You got a damn Cadillac because that was symbolic of making it 	in 	America 	was
finally having a Cadillac in the driveway.
Symbols matter to
people.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 34 of 37 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	35
┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
Now that has fallen apart and gone away. It doesn't matter anymore much one
way or the other, but there are these symbols that matter to people.
Ideas that stick by 	graphic and dramatic images. 	Everybody knows the car
crash commercials with the dummy and the thing crashes into the wall.
If you're of a certain age, you know one of the first successful over-the-counter
drug commercials was Anacin, 	which had a hammer inside a person's head,
pounding away. It's a great graphic image.
In direct sales, my 	all-time favorite, and it is oldÔÇŽ 	however, 	they only stopped
using this about 	eight 	or 	nine 	years ago; 	in the fire alarm business it's famous.
It's a fire alarm video, so when the fire alarm salesman came 	ÔÇô 	and 	still in some
market areas comes 	ÔÇô 	to 	your house to sell you fire alarms, 	they show you this
film. They sit there with you at the kitchen table so you watch the film.
Driving people to it on a webcast, by the way, doesn't work at all because they
stop watching. 	It's a very slow-paced build to 	one whack at the end. It's called,
in the industry, 	the 	ÔÇťCrying 	DogÔÇŁ 	film.
It's ponderous, but it is a very powerful image. 	Roughly a third of the people
who see it in the sales environment tear up.
The spouse looks at the other one and the tears are coming
out of their eyes too, and it clinches the fire alarm sale.
The fire alarm business, by the way, is about $3,000 to $8,000 sales and they're
heat detectors.
The 	three-minute explanation about that, you all have smoke detectors. 	Here,
we are not protected by smoke detectors. If a fire breaks out in here, sprinklers
are going to go on. They're detected by heat, they're not detected by smoke. If
they wait to be detected by smoke, we all burn to death.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 35 of 37 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	36
┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
Contrary to what the smoke detector guys tell you, 	two 	out of 	three 	fires reach
maximum heat before they generate much smoke, so these are heat detectors.
What happens is, when they detect a certain amount of heat, they pop off, 	which
triggers the sprinkler system. A home heat detector is a metal can; you could go
home and build one. It's a round, metal can, about the size of a smoke detector,
and it mounts on the wall.
What's inside it is a big, coiled metal spring. It's a
mechanical device 	ÔÇô 	no batteries; 	no electric, no
nothing.
When a certain amount of heat happens, the
outside 	pops off, 	which lets the coiled spring
loose, which unwinds in the can making a hell of a racket.
That's what a heat detector is, and they're sold for $3,000 to $8,000 to do an
average size house. 	That's what this business is and it's a very profitable
business, by the way, because it's a metal can with a coiled spring inside it.
At one time, this was a huge industry in America. Direct selling is less, and the
smoke detectors became a problem and all of that, but it still exists. There's two
companies. They're fairly substantial. They probably 	each 	have 10,000 dealers
out in the market place.
They each have 	four or five 	salespeople. So there 	are 	probably 50,000 people in
this business today, still going to somebody's house at night and in some cases,
showing the more modern version of the 	Crying 	Dog 	film. 	The new one is
nowhere near as good as the original.
Dramatic images are very, very powerful and we tend to neglect them in
producing 	video 	today, 	doing 	webcasts 	today, 	even 	doing 	live 	audience
presentations 	ÔÇô 	in 	much the same way, that in print, we tend not to pay as much
attention to the photographs as we should.
Dramatic images are
very, very powerful
and we tend to
neglect them.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 36 of 37 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	37
┬ę 2014 	GKIC 	Module 1
Print is driven by copywriters and so we really think about the language more
than we think about the photographic images.
Video tends to be driven by the scriptwriters in direct response. We think about
the scripts, not as much about the images, but these 	images are very, very
powerful.
www.getwsodownload.com
